reviewers.primary_use_case: evidence retrieval and evidence-grounded audit interface prototyping
reviewers.summary: The corpus is most useful for finding and ranking the policy passages that auditors should inspect, not for automatically deciding app compliance.

reviewers.primary_use_case: release risk assessment and responsible dataset governance
reviewers.summary: Release is acceptable if reviewer judgments are not framed as legal compliance findings and if discouraged uses are explicit.

schema_feedback.comment: The evidence excerpt must remain paired with the structured claim; otherwise users will misinterpret sentence-level evidence.

benchmark_feedback.baseline_expectation: Simple lexical baselines are appropriate as floors; LLM systems should beat top-K retrieval and show calibrated uncertainty before UI deployment.

discouraged_uses: training end-to-end compliance classifiers without annotator-aware evaluation

affordances_to_avoid: automatic red mismatch badge from keyword hits

paper_framing_feedback.best_framing: The corpus is an HCI record of citation behaviour by trained reviewers.
paper_framing_feedback.secondary_framing: The corpus is a benchmark for evidence-locator models.
paper_framing_feedback.weak_framing: The corpus is a compliance ground truth dataset.
paper_framing_feedback.recommended_claim: The corpus supports evidence-grounded review interfaces by showing what policy text human auditors cite for each structured disclosure claim.

direct_quotes: I would use this corpus primarily for evidence retrieval and interface prototyping, not for end-to-end compliance classification.
The biggest risk is that external users treat a human audit verdict as a legal finding.
The evidence excerpt must remain paired with the structured claim; a sentence alone is not enough.
